transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hello.
Hi, everyone.
How are you?
you?
Hello.
Hello.
I'm going to share our agenda.
Okay.
I'll just jump in if that works for you all since I know we've got a lot to cover in 30 minutes.
So I wanted to let you know that we have seven articles ready to publish and should be starting tomorrow.
We just got kind of backed up in publishing when reviews came through.
So our plan is to publish a few tomorrow to catch up and then go back to the one a day just to make sure we're publishing at the right weekly cadence.
I did want to discuss shifting to...
would know start So

And publishing in batches, you know, if we have up to five ready at once in a week, publishing them all in one day instead of publishing daily, I think from an operations perspective, it would be smoother.

And I think the other thing is that it sort of, you sort of gain four or five days on a piece of content indexing because, you know, if you're publishing it all on Monday instead of waiting to publish that last one on Friday, like it's incremental gains, but it's also just sort of gains you leave on the table if you have it ready to go and you're choosing not to publish it.
And I think if the worry was sort of how it would look, you know, on your blog homepage to have them all there at once, that's sort of not how people discover content.
I think if you look at traffic to blog homepages, and I can kind of confirm this with yours, it's traditionally extremely low.
Because, you know, if you think of the way you consume content, you tend not to, like, go to a brand's blog homepage and then kind of search through their library.
Usually you've come through a specific query.
So I know it's not, it was like a choice to publish once a day, but I did want to sort of propose this alternative model if you were open to it.
I mean, it's not a big deal.
It sounds like it's more complicated to do one a day.
We haven't really been able to do it consistently.
So for one reason or another, so it's fine.
We can do five in a day.
And I think I would like to see a little bit more variety on the, on the, on the authors for sure.
Yeah.
So I wanted to ask who, do you have additional author profiles set up in Webflow that we should rotate between?
Or is that something that you want to create?
I don't know.
don't know.
thanks.
I don't know, what tool do we have, additional profiles?
We have a few, but that's Shakur, Vikesh, Kiran, Jody.
Nah, Jody, yes.
Kiran, yes.
We have a couple of BDRs, too.
I mean, we should create one for all the SEs.
SEs, Customer Success.
CSMs, yeah.
P, I would leave Shakur and Vikesh alone, unless it's a special, special blog.
And, yeah, those, those are the ones.
Okay.
So if you want to set those up in Webflow, and then just give us, you can just drop it in Slack, some guidance.
If you have, like, if you're, if there's specific subject matter experts, and you have, like, certain blog posts, you want us to attach to their name, or whether you want us to just, um, rotate.
Let us know kind of what approach you want, and then, you know, we're happy to do that.
Okay.
Awesome.
Okay.
And then in terms of some discussion items I wanted to touch base on around the content, one, I want to say that we're going to test picking up that versus lane of content again next week.
I think there was a lot of good info in the messaging and positioning doc you shared, and Marianna said she's also been getting better results with some tweaks to the research step of the pipeline.
So we're going to try that one again and submit it and see what you think.
Hopefully that'll be kind of a richer and better positioned, but we'd love to kind of be able to prioritize those again.
So we'll use this as a task to see whether we need more info from you on positioning or whether it feels like we're good to go here.
right.
All
I was going to ask if we've been publishing a few white papers, I was wondering if those would be beneficial to your pipeline in terms of training the model.
Should we send those your way?
You can send them to us.
They might be.

It depends a little bit, I think, on the content of them, but they absolutely might be, and especially, I think, if they contain any kind of unique insights or, you know, unique research of any kind, things that we could reference.
I think they're more like, they're good in terms of, like, the definition of our category, agent of physical security, and the definition of capabilities of our models, like the VLM.
That's kind of what we have right now.
But I think in terms of, like, setting up certain definitions of how we refer to things, they could be useful.
So we'll send them to you.
Yeah, send them to us, because I know one thing Marian is building out is that glossary, just to make sure she's approaching, especially with those branded terms.
Yeah.
I like
Definitely they could inform that, and I'm sure there's probably a lot of other useful insights in there.
There's also a little bit of competitive positioning embedded into that, so it's also helpful.
Those would definitely be helpful to pull out for those versus ones.
And I think, yeah, in general, I usually tell clients to just send us everything you have, because we can pull out whatever is useful.

And sometimes white papers are actually super useful to, you know, if they're tied closely enough to SEO content, to break up, you know, and use to write specific blog posts kind of on the same topic.
It sort of just depends a little bit on what the content is on.
So, yeah, we would love to take a look, so send them our way.
Okay.
The other thing I wanted to touch based on was just aligning around some edits.
I think some edits have come through, and Marianna mentioned.
And things like where a post, an educational post will mention threat detection, changing it to ambient threat detection.
And I just want to call out for these educational posts, it's really important to balance brand mentions.
But for educational top of funnel content, our approach is really to tie it to ambient in the closing section.
Because if you use these branded terms throughout the entire post, it can sort of be interpreted as salesy, and it can actually hurt SEO and the ability for the content to rank.
So if you see that not being mentioned at the top, and it is an ambient function, it's not an oversight.
It's, you know, you can always confirm if it doesn't seem right.
But for educational top of funnel posts, it is sort of a choice that we've made to balance this and give the content a better chance to rank.
Yeah.
I mean, I guess it depends on the context.
I don't know if I made those changes or someone else.
What I did find, what we did find in the past was that sometimes you would describe the general capability of threat detection with the specificity of what Ambient does and is unique to us.
In terms of generalized, you would say like AI platform can give you threat detection that has hundreds of signatures, right?
I'm giving you an example.
That is not true.
Like that is just the Ambient AI platform that can give you hundreds.
So it depends on the context.
And by the way, when you put Ambient in front of threat detection, it needs to be capitalized because it's the name of our product.
That was me, not Mariana.
So that's probably why.
No, that's fine.
So that's a detail, a secondary detail, but the primary one is it's okay to generalize as long as it's in the right context and it's not generalizing our differentiators to everybody.
Yeah, 100%.
So it'd be great to say, well, again, AI platform will allow you to capture several type of threats.
Ambient threat detection gives you the largest library, 150 plus.
See what mean?
So you got to do it that way.
I don't know where it was.
Maybe this wasn't the case, but that's the caveat.
No, I think that is a really important context.
And I'll go back to Mariana with that feedback too, just to make sure if we're talking about the general concept, we are.
Defining it and not defining the general concept using kind of your differentiators as a brand.
So that makes a lot of sense.
And the other question that we had was around, there were sort of some terms that I think had been deprioritized or that you didn't want to work on.
think spatial intelligence was one, contextual AI.
AI, we're wondering, you know, if these are sort of have search volume, are you kind of open to expanding into these?
Or even if it's sort of not specifically the way Ambient talks about it as part of your brand, if these related terms have search value, does that make sense?
Yeah, so I'm not familiar with spatial intelligence and what was the origin.
I've never, since I've been with the company, that term has never been used.
Do you know a tool?
No, I think it's from before, like three versions before, like in 2019.
So, okay, so in that case, I'm talking about spatial intelligence.
I think there may be the opportunity to have a piece that says this is how spatial intelligence has evolved.
Frankly, I don't know what we refer to as spatial intelligence.
So we would have to investigate, but it can't be the primary, you know, a very common term that we use.
It's not part of our language anymore.
Okay.
I think contextual AI, okay, we use context all the time, is the new, for our messaging, the new focus is on reasoning AI.
has to your Bye.
I don't know.
I mean, it kind of makes sense.
It's not bad, but it's also not the one that I would prioritize all the time.

Yeah, I think it's more a question around, you know, if we find these related terms that have, you know, a high search volume and seem like a good opportunity, are you open to us, you know, proposing them and the proposed topics?
Yeah, that's fine.
But I also wouldn't want to do a whole blog series with tens of assets just on that, you know, so we can calibrate the volume.
Maybe you do one or two pieces and that's it.

Yeah, I think definitely, I think it's much more just around making sure we put the right guardrails around our keyword research to make sure we're not proposing things that are lanes you want us to go down.
Albert, I just checked the previous assets.
Spatial intelligence was used in 2019 when we were talking about occupancy insights.
Oh, then, yeah, then, no.
We don't do it anymore.
Maybe we'll resurrect it if we go back to do occupancy.
I mean, the capability exists, right?
We just don't focus it on anymore, I guess, is the point.
You are right.
There's some level of it.
I think we have it.
We just haven't invested.
So we kind of stayed pretty basic.
I mean, it's pretty much just counting how many people are in a room at a particular specific time.
I would not prioritize it right now.
I think it has some flaws.
Like, it's not good.
Yeah.
Okay, well, we'll do prioritize it for now.
I think it was much more just flagging these sort of related opportunities and making sure you're okay with us chasing them when they feel, you know, relevant.
And valuable for me.
From a search intent perspective.

Maybe it's something that we can rebrand or repurpose when it comes to talking about spaces like museums, where we're talking about people touching art, where the system needs to be aware of where the art pieces are kept and how people interact with them.
All right, we need to resolve it internally first and then come back and determine what to do here.
I don't think we have an answer.
Thanks, mate.
Okay.
In terms of reporting this week, just for a snapshot, I want you to know that we updated the Looker to filter out the traffic that you were seeing, the spikes coming from Asia.
So this is just U.S.?
Yeah.
U.S., I think the markets that you listed, was U.S., New Zealand.
I can't remember what the other two were.
But it's filtered to the markets.

That you listed, and if you actually, you can't see it here in a screenshot, but there is a toggle, and you can actually go through in the checkbox and, like, check and uncheck which countries, which markets you'd like to see or not see as well.
So this is just the view of the markets you did want to see.
Good, because all our dashboards in GA4 are broken because of China.
Yeah, so if you go into Looker now, you should be, you can, and you'll see what countries are checked, but you have the ability to check and uncheck them.
So you can also go in there if you want to play around with it as well.
So this is, we're seeing, you know, strong kind of momentum in the content program.

And in terms of organic search, I think we talked about this a bit the other week, but we did see, like, an initial jump in queries, and then I think in December and into January, it feels like it's plateaued a little at that top, the sort of 2 to 10.
So the footprint is continuing to grow, but we haven't seen a lot of movement at the top.

So I think a priority for the next step is for us to identify, I think, some queries that seem to be hovering in that range and see if they're good candidates for on-page optimization or refreshing the content to see if we can experiment a little and see what's going to push it into that kind of page one position.
So that's something we're going to start on next week.

In terms of top-performing content, we found the AI threat detection and then the competitive comparison content are driving the strongest results, which is another reason we want to pick back up that versus series of articles, because I think that has the potential to drive strong results there.
In terms of impression, there was something I wanted to flag for you.
So we're seeing...
We're seeing...
So...
A positive trend in terms of the GrowthX content, where impressions are kind of heading in the direction we want, growing month over month.
I did see that site-wide, the impressions declined in January.
And I wouldn't say this is, I think it's worth keeping an eye on, because your clicks didn't change.
So it could be that you're just losing kind of low-value queries, or you're getting more high-intent traffic.
So it could just be that the overall composition isn't changing in a way that matters.

But I think something worth keeping an eye on, especially sort of if you didn't have any technical changes, or like if there weren't any specific changes that might have triggered this, I think something that we'll continue to track month over month.
And I guess that is an open question, if there's, if you feel like there was anything.
Anything technical that might have changed.
When did we change to Webflow?
Could it be that, that we changed to website platform?
I can look and see exactly what day.
Do you remember the date?
I mean, that was in December, right?
Yeah.
It was early December.
I think so.
Second or third.
I can see and dig into it more.
I'll go dig into the week over week and see if it seemed like there was a more steep decline there.
But I think it was more gradual.
Like, I wouldn't, it didn't seem like there was a specific event.
But I can dig into that a little more with that in mind, knowing that you did make that change.
Because it was right before I came on the account.
So I should be, I'll dig up that date and I'll take a look and follow up with that.
But if that was still inflection.
Thank Thank
Wouldn't December also have seen a dip?
Because the change happened on December 4th.
Yeah, that would be my assumption, but it's also possible that there could be more than one thing could be true, could be going on, right?
But like I said, it doesn't feel because the clicks have maintained relatively stable, they haven't declined proportionally.
It can also just be like dropping low-value queries, like that's something we've seen as well.
So I think it also might be worth me digging in an audit, just doing an audit in terms of which pages lost impressions, because I think that will tell us a lot too.
If it's a bunch of low-value pages, then it doesn't really matter.
If the ones that lost impressions, I can go into the Google Search Console and see, then we'll kind of have a sense of whether it's an issue.
So I will follow up on that after this and take a look.
One more question about this.
Does impression count also take into consideration the traffic coming in from the ads we run?
No, this is organic traffic.
All right.
So this won't be affected by that.
And then just in terms of other performance, know you have that kickoff meeting coming in mid-February.

So next week, our plan is to kind of deliver you that just overview, both the normal kind of monthly overview for January as well as kind of an overview on the performance of the content program so far.
Just taking a look at, you know, what we've seen so far, where we've seen growth, and also identifying other potential places.
Where we could lean in either, you know, doubling down on certain clusters or experimenting with other types of content.
My ask was to sort of take a look at where we're seeing movement for other clients.
Especially kind of experimenting with other types of content and see if any of it feels like it would be a good fit for Ambient as well.
So we're going to get that to you next week so you have time to take a look and hopefully, you know, ask us any questions.
Ahead of that, ahead of that meeting.
I would like to do the goal planning session way sooner than after sales kickoff.
In fact, it would have been valuable for us to have done it already because next week I have an executive strategy meeting.
So I'm sure we'll get the questions.
But yeah, certainly before sales kickoff.

Or either next week or at most a week after, I'd like to go into sales kickoff with a plan to basically tell the rest of the company, this on the SEO side and content side, we're going to drive in this direction.
Okay, I think we can get that on the calendar for next week.
Does this time slot work for you next week?
No, I'll be tied up in Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, all day, so I can't in the strategy meeting.
So I can only do either Monday or Tuesday.

If you just want to send a couple blocks of time that you're free, we can try and make them work, and if I can move some stuff around as well, if you just want to send a couple options, either for next week or the following, whatever works better for you.
Okay.
Okay.
Next week, we're also rolling out the AI visibility tool that I've been mentioning to clients.
So we finally have an actual date.
So I will be able to demo that for you.
And then we're also going to get you log in.
So you'll be able to go in as well and do things like edit your prompts, see where you're being cited and how you're appearing, and can kind of get in there and add your own prompts as well.
And then also just kind of peek under the hood to see what we're seeing to build strategy.
Because I know not having access to Scrunch has been hard to actually kind of see some of that.
So next week, you should have access.
Okay.
Sounds cool.
Awesome.
All right.

time.

so much.
